policy_statement: NAP target: reduce the share of the continental coastline in a critical state of erosion from 16.5% (2020 target) to 10% by 2030.
mechanism_description: The policy sets a target to reduce critical coastal erosion, which supports shoreline stability and improves resilience to sea-level rise and storm impacts.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (sea-level rise + storms) → coastal erosion risk → erosion management → ↓ shoreline change/↑ protective capacity → ↓ exposure & vulnerability → ↓ climate impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system: Coastal zone management actions are implemented to reduce the extent of coastline in critical erosion condition (e.g., shoreline stabilization, sediment management, dune/beach reinforcement, setbacks).  
(2) Mediating process: Physical coastal processes are altered—sediment budgets, wave energy dissipation, and geomorphic stability—so shorelines erode more slowly and maintain protective features (beaches/dunes/cliffs) that buffer surge and waves.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced damages and service disruption from sea-level rise and storm events via lower erosion rates, maintained coastal protection, and improved resilience of coastal communities and assets.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced exposure to sea-level rise and storm-driven coastal flooding/erosion damages through increased shoreline stability.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts on coastal geomorphology and shoreline stability to reduce harm from climate-amplified coastal hazards (SLR, storms), i.e., impact reduction rather than emissions reduction.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The core intended outcome is resilience (reduced erosion-related risk and damages). Any GHG effects are secondary and highly implementation-dependent.  
3) Edge cases applied: If implemented via nature-based measures (dunes, wetlands), there may be Nature-Based Solutions co-benefits; if implemented via material-intensive hard defenses, there may be added emissions—neither changes the dominant adaptation purpose/effect for the mechanism class.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is “manage/reduce critical coastal erosion to improve coastal resilience” principally reduces exposure/vulnerability to coastal hazards, fitting Adaptation consistently across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype note: Specific subtypes (e.g., nature-based vs. engineered protection vs. managed retreat) and co-benefits/harms require policy-level evidence and are deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some erosion management is achieved through ecosystem-based approaches (could also be framed as Nature-Based Solutions) or through hard armoring (may increase embodied emissions and shift erosion downdrift). Classification remains Adaptation because the dominant pathway is reduction of climate impact risk; co-benefits/tradeoffs depend on implementation details.
key_indicators: “NAP target”; “reduce the share of the continental coastline in a critical state of erosion”; “from 16.5% … to 10% by 2030”; “critical state of erosion | 16,5% | 10%”
co_benefits: Potential coastal ecosystem protection, tourism/beach amenity preservation, and reduced non-climate coastal hazard losses (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Target implies subsequent measures (e.g., coastal works, zoning), but the text itself contains only an indicator/target, so classified as target-setting rather than infrastructure investment or regulation.
